questions.question: Article 38.1 provides that 'no person shall be tried on any criminal charge save in due course of law'. What does 'due course of law' require, according to the Supreme Court?

options.A: Only that the trial follows the procedure laid down in the relevant statute
options.B: That a criminal trial must comply with constitutional justice — including fair procedures, an independent tribunal, the presumption of innocence, the right to a fair trial, and the other guarantees in Art. 38; 'due course of law' incorporates all constitutional requirements for a valid criminal trial
options.C: Only that the accused is represented by a solicitor at all stages of the criminal process
options.D: Only that the trial is conducted in the Irish language

questions.explanation: 'Due course of law' in Art. 38.1 has been interpreted broadly by the courts to encompass the totality of constitutional requirements for a valid criminal trial. It imports the principles of natural and constitutional justice, the presumption of innocence, fair procedures, the right to an independent and impartial tribunal, and the other specific guarantees in Art. 38 (trial by jury for non-minor offences, right to legal representation in serious cases, prohibition on retrospective offences, etc.). It is the constitutional foundation for the entire body of criminal procedural rights in Irish law.

questions.question: In Hardy v Ireland [1994] 2 IR 550, the accused challenged the constitutionality of provisions of the Criminal Evidence Act 1992 that modified the rules of evidence in criminal trials. The Supreme Court considered the standard for assessing such modifications. What was held?

options.A: Any statutory modification of criminal evidence rules automatically violates Art. 38.1
options.B: The Oireachtas may modify rules of criminal evidence provided the overall trial remains fair and meets the Art. 38.1 'due course of law' standard; individual modifications must not deprive the accused of a fair trial in the round
options.C: The Criminal Evidence Act 1992 was declared unconstitutional in its entirety
options.D: Art. 38.1 requires all criminal evidence rules to be identical to those at common law in 1937

questions.explanation: Hardy v Ireland [1994] 2 IR 550 confirmed that the Oireachtas may modify criminal evidence rules — including departing from strict common law rules — provided the overall fairness of the trial is maintained. The constitutional test under Art. 38.1 is whether the accused receives a trial 'in due course of law' — i.e., a fair trial with all essential constitutional protections. This is an overall assessment, not a rule-by-rule analysis. Individual modifications that improve efficiency or protect vulnerable witnesses can be permissible if the accused retains a fair opportunity to defend themselves.

questions.question: Article 38.2 provides that 'minor offences may be tried by courts of summary jurisdiction'. Which of the following most accurately describes the constitutional test for determining whether an offence is 'minor' (and thus triable without jury)?

options.A: Any offence carrying a maximum penalty of less than 12 months imprisonment is automatically minor
options.B: The 'minor offence' test considers the nature of the penalty (severity), the moral quality of the act, and whether the offence is one that was historically treated as minor; the Supreme Court has indicated that offences carrying potential imprisonment of up to 12 months and moderate fines are generally minor, but severity of potential penalty is a key indicator
options.C: An offence is minor if and only if the accused elects for summary trial
options.D: The classification of offences as minor or non-minor is entirely a matter for the Oireachtas, beyond constitutional review

questions.explanation: The constitutional 'minor offence' test was established in Melling v O'Mathghamhna [1962] IR 1 and developed in subsequent cases including Conroy v AG [1965] IR 411. The test is multi-factorial: courts consider the nature of the moral stigma attaching to conviction, the severity of the potential penalty (both fine and imprisonment), and the classification of the offence historically. Generally, offences carrying up to 12 months imprisonment and proportionate fines are considered minor. Non-minor offences must be tried by jury under Art. 38.5, unless tried by the Special Criminal Court (Art. 38.3) or a military tribunal (Art. 38.4).

questions.question: Article 38.5 provides that every person charged with a serious offence has the right to trial by jury. In O'Callaghan v Attorney General [1993] 2 IR 17, the Supreme Court considered the relationship between this right and the District Court's jurisdiction in minor offences. What principle was established?

options.A: The right to trial by jury can be waived by the accused in all cases
options.B: The right to trial by jury for serious offences is a constitutional right that cannot be diminished by statute; the classification of an offence as 'minor' to avoid jury trial cannot be used to circumvent the constitutional right for genuinely serious offences
options.C: The Oireachtas has absolute discretion to classify any offence as minor to avoid jury trial requirements
options.D: The right to trial by jury applies only to offences created after 1937

questions.explanation: The constitutional right to trial by jury under Art. 38.5 for non-minor offences is a fundamental procedural guarantee. Courts scrutinise attempts to classify as 'minor' offences that carry substantial penalties or significant moral stigma. The Oireachtas cannot circumvent the jury trial right simply by labelling a serious offence as 'minor'. The courts apply the constitutional minor offence test (Melling; Conroy) to ensure that offences genuinely warranting jury trial — because of their severity or nature — are not reclassified to avoid Art. 38.5. This limits legislative flexibility but protects the accused's constitutional right.

questions.question: In The People (DPP) v DO'T [2003] 4 IR 286, the Supreme Court considered the admissibility of a statement made by an accused after extensive questioning, where the accused had limited intellectual capacity. What was the constitutional dimension?

options.A: Statements made by an accused are always admissible regardless of intellectual capacity
options.B: Art. 38.1 requires that a criminal trial be fair to the particular accused; a statement obtained in circumstances where the accused could not meaningfully exercise their rights (due to intellectual disability, lack of understanding of caution, or undue pressure) may be inadmissible as the admission of such evidence would render the trial unfair
options.C: Only physical evidence is subject to Art. 38.1 fairness requirements; confessions are governed solely by the common law voluntariness test
options.D: The constitutional test requires that all accused persons have the same IQ to ensure equal trials

questions.explanation: Art. 38.1's requirement of a trial 'in due course of law' includes the requirement that the trial be fair to the individual accused. Where an accused has limited intellectual capacity, the court must assess whether they genuinely understood the caution, could exercise their right to silence, and whether any statement was truly voluntary in a meaningful sense. Admitting a statement obtained in circumstances where the accused could not meaningfully exercise constitutional rights may violate Art. 38.1. Courts have discretion to exclude such evidence where admission would render the trial unfair.

questions.question: Article 38.1 requires trial 'in due course of law'. Does this impose any obligations on the prosecution beyond procedural fairness?

options.A: No — Art. 38.1 only imposes procedural requirements on the court conducting the trial
options.B: Yes — Art. 38.1 requires that the prosecution act fairly and in good faith; this includes the duty to disclose relevant evidence to the defence (disclosure obligations), the prohibition on non-disclosure of exculpatory material, and restrictions on prosecutorial misconduct that would undermine a fair trial
options.C: Art. 38.1 imposes no obligations on the prosecution — only on the judge
options.D: Art. 38.1 only applies to the Garda Síochána, not to the DPP

questions.explanation: Art. 38.1's fair trial guarantee imposes obligations on the prosecution as well as the court. The DPP has a constitutional and common law duty to disclose all evidence relevant to the defence — including exculpatory material — in order to ensure a fair trial. Non-disclosure of material evidence can violate Art. 38.1 and may result in the quashing of a conviction or a stay on proceedings. This duty of disclosure is fundamental to the adversarial system and the constitutional guarantee of a fair trial. The obligation is ongoing throughout the criminal process.

questions.question: Article 38.1 prohibits retrospective criminal offences ('nullum crimen sine lege'). Which provision of the Constitution most directly addresses this principle?

options.A: Art. 34 (courts) provides the prohibition on retrospective offences
options.B: Art. 15.5.1° provides that the Oireachtas shall not declare acts to be infringements of the law which were not so at the date of their commission; this is the express constitutional prohibition on retrospective criminal legislation, complementing the Art. 38.1 fair trial guarantee
options.C: Art. 40.4.1° (personal liberty) is the only provision prohibiting retrospective criminal law
options.D: The prohibition on retrospective crimes is purely a common law principle with no express constitutional basis

questions.explanation: Art. 15.5.1° expressly provides that the Oireachtas shall not declare acts to be infringements of the law which were not so at the date of their commission. This is the constitutional prohibition on retrospective criminal legislation (nullum crimen sine lege — no crime without a prior law). It complements Art. 38.1's fair trial guarantee: a person cannot be tried for conduct that was not criminal when performed. The prohibition is an aspect of the rule of law and the principle that individuals must be able to know in advance what conduct is prohibited. Art. 15.5.2° similarly prohibits retrospective increases in criminal penalties.

questions.question: In State (Healy) v Donoghue [1976] IR 325, what specific right was recognised as part of the Art. 38.1 guarantee in serious criminal proceedings?

options.B: The right to legal representation — where an accused facing a serious criminal charge cannot afford a solicitor, the State must provide one at public expense; failure to do so can violate Art. 38.1 and Art. 40.3
options.C: The right to trial in the Irish language
options.D: The right to an automatic appeal to the Court of Appeal

questions.explanation: State (Healy) v Donoghue [1976] IR 325 recognised the constitutional right to legal representation in serious criminal proceedings as part of Art. 38.1's 'due course of law' guarantee and Art. 40.3's protection of personal rights. Where an accused faces serious charges (particularly involving potential imprisonment) and cannot afford legal representation, the State has a constitutional obligation to provide legal aid. This led to the Criminal Justice (Legal Aid) Act 1962 (previously in force) and the subsequent expansion of the legal aid scheme. The right is not to any particular lawyer but to the provision of adequate legal representation.

questions.question: Article 38.3 permits the establishment of special courts for the trial of offences where the ordinary courts are inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice and the preservation of public peace and order. What procedural safeguard does Art. 38.3.2° provide regarding such courts?

options.A: Art. 38.3.2° requires that special courts be presided over by Supreme Court judges
options.B: Art. 38.3.1° requires that special courts be established only by law; the Constitution does not expressly specify the procedural safeguards for special courts beyond this, but the courts have held that Art. 38.1 still applies — accused before the Special Criminal Court must still be tried 'in due course of law'
options.C: Art. 38.3.2° requires mandatory jury trials in special courts
options.D: Art. 38.3 provides that special courts may only try capital offences

questions.explanation: Art. 38.3.1° permits special courts to be established by law for cases where the ordinary courts are 'inadequate to secure the effective administration of justice and the preservation of public peace and order'. The Special Criminal Court (SCC) was established under the Offences Against the State Act 1939. While the SCC sits without a jury (three judges), Art. 38.1 still applies — trials before the SCC must still be conducted 'in due course of law', meaning with fair procedures, the rules of evidence (as modified for the SCC context), the right to legal representation, and other constitutional guarantees. The absence of a jury does not exempt the SCC from Art. 38.1.

questions.question: Can the right to trial by jury under Art. 38.5 be validly waived by an accused person?

options.A: No — the right to jury trial is absolute and cannot be waived
options.B: In limited circumstances, an accused may consent to trial without jury — e.g., summary trial of offences that are not genuinely 'non-minor'; however, the accused cannot validly waive the constitutional right to jury trial where the offence is genuinely non-minor, as the right exists for reasons of public interest not just individual protection
options.C: Yes — any accused may waive jury trial at any stage simply by filing a written waiver with the court
options.D: Yes — Irish law permits judge-only trials for all indictable offences by agreement of the accused and the DPP

questions.explanation: The position on waiver of the Art. 38.5 jury trial right is complex. For non-minor offences, the jury trial right exists in part for reasons of public interest in lay participation in justice, not merely individual protection. This suggests the right cannot be entirely waived by an accused alone. The Criminal Law (Jury Trials) Act has not been enacted (various proposals have been rejected). In practice, judge-only trials in Ireland are confined to the Special Criminal Court (Art. 38.3), military tribunals (Art. 38.4), and the Central Criminal Court jury waiver in limited circumstances under specific legislation. Waiver must be fully informed and voluntary.
